summary: Gruber explores how Apple might handle the rumored transition to a taller 1136×640 iPhone display at WWDC while maintaining secrecy about unannounced hardware. He suggests Apple could introduce WebOS-style stacking notification banners in iOS 6 that shrink app content, which would naturally encourage developers to build flexible layouts — preparing them for a taller screen without explicitly revealing it. He argues the display change would keep the same device width but stretch vertically to 16:9 by shrinking the bezel, and forcefully rejects the popular theory that extra pixels would house a persistent app-switching dock, calling it antithetical to iOS design and a usability disaster.
key_points: Apple could use iOS 6 notification banner changes to quietly encourage developers to build height-flexible apps, preparing them for a taller display without revealing hardware plans
iOS apps already handle vertical resizing via double-height status bars, so a taller screen isn't a fundamentally new paradigm for developers
A 4-inch display could fit in the same physical device by shrinking the non-functional bezel areas above and below the screen
A persistent app-switching dock would violate iOS's core design principle that apps own the full display, and would cause constant accidental taps near the keyboard
The design tension in smartphones is between wanting a bigger screen and a smaller device — stretching vertically while maintaining width threads that needle
money_quotes: Apple says "Jump"; iOS developers ask "How high?"
The design tension in post-iPhone mobile phones is between screen size (where bigger is better) and device size (where smaller is better).
When you launch an app, the device, conceptually, is the app. A persistent app dock would ruin that.
Miss the space bar by a few pixels and pop — you're zapped to another app, mid-sentence.
If Apple's designers think 16:9 would be better today, they'll switch. It's that simple.
I do not expect them to increase the physical size of the device itself.
Too soon to speak of WebOS in the past tense?
key_insight: Apple could use iOS 6 software changes like stackable notification banners to stealth-prepare developers for a taller iPhone display without ever mentioning unannounced hardware.
